MODULE 3 · Hands-On with Microsoft Copilot

03

Module 3: Hands-On with Microsoft Copilot

Introduction

You now understand that AI is a "prediction engine" - a Super Parrot that generates plausible text without actually knowing if it is true. That knowledge is essential. But theory only gets you so far.

It is time to take the tool for a test drive.

For this course, we will use Microsoft Copilot, as your institution provides access to a protected version. However, the principles we cover apply to any generative AI tool - ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or institutional chatbots. The interfaces differ, but the habits you need to develop are the same.

Safety First

There are two versions of most AI tools:

Version

Your data

Who can see it

Public/Free

May be stored, analysed, and used to train future AI models

Potentially anyone

Protected/Enterprise

Stays within your organisation's security boundary

Only you (and your IT team's logs)

If you use a public version and paste in student names, grades, research data, or sensitive meeting notes, that information could end up anywhere. This is not hypothetical - there have been documented cases of training data appearing in other users' outputs.

Your institution has provided a protected version specifically to avoid this risk. But you must verify you are using it.

Your institution has provided a protected version specifically to avoid this risk. But you must verify you are using it every time you open the tool.

The Green Shield Check

Before you type a single character of work-related content:

  1. Open Copilot (or your institution's AI tool)
  2. Look for a security indicator - typically in the top-right corner or near your profile
  3. Do you see a "Protected" badge, shield icon, or enterprise indicator?

What you see

What it means

What to do

✅ Green shield / "Protected" / Enterprise badge

You are logged in with your work account. Data stays within your organisation.

Proceed with appropriate caution (still follow the 'Red Lines' - see Module 4)

⚠️ No indicator / "Free" / Personal account shown

You may be using the public web version

Stop. Log out and sign in with your institutional credentials

❓ Unsure

The interface may have changed

Check with your IT/Digital team before inputting any work data

Make this check a habit. Every time you open the tool. Every time you switch devices. It takes two seconds and prevents serious data breaches.

Think about the devices and accounts you currently use for AI tools. Do you know whether you are using a protected version? What would you need to check or change?

What If Your Institution Uses a Different Tool?

The same principle applies regardless of which AI tool you use:

  • ChatGPT: Look for "ChatGPT Enterprise" or "ChatGPT Team" indicator
  • Claude: Check for organisation name in the interface
  • Google Gemini: Verify you are in your Workspace account, not personal Google
  • Institutional chatbot: These are typically protected by design, but confirm with IT

The rule is universal: verify your protected status before inputting work data.

Modes and Habits

Standard Mode vs. Reasoning Mode

Many AI tools now offer different "thinking" modes. Understanding these helps you choose the right approach for each task.

Standard Mode (Fast Response)

What it is: The default mode. The AI responds quickly using its standard prediction process.

Speed: Near-instant (starts generating within 1-2 seconds)

Best for:

  • Drafting emails and routine correspondence
  • Summarising text you have provided
  • Explaining concepts in simpler terms
  • Brainstorming ideas and options
  • Reformatting or restructuring content

Characteristics: Good for volume, may be more superficial on complex problems.

"Think Deeper" / Reasoning Mode

What it is: The AI takes longer to "reason" through the problem before responding. It may show its working or thinking process.

Speed: Slower (you may see a "Thinking..." animation for 10-30 seconds)

Best for:

  • Complex analysis or multi-step problems
  • Comparing options with trade-offs
  • Planning or structuring a large piece of work
  • Tasks requiring logical reasoning
  • When your first attempt got a superficial answer

Characteristics: More thorough, but uses more computing resources (may have daily limits).

Activity: The Jargon Buster

Try the following prompt in your institution's AI tool: "Explain 'pedagogical scaffolding' to me as if I am a new student who has never studied education theory." Then try a variation: "Explain 'pedagogical scaffolding' in exactly three bullet points, suitable for a staff induction handbook." Notice how the output changes when you specify a format, a length constraint, and an audience. This is a preview of the prompting techniques we will cover in Module 6.

The Fresh Start Habit

When you chat with an AI, it remembers everything in that conversation. This can cause problems when you switch topics - the AI may weirdly blend previous context into a new task, or errors from earlier may compound. The rule is simple: New Task = New Chat.

When you are finished with one task and starting something different, look for a "New Chat", "New Conversation", or "+" button and click it to clear the AI's memory of your previous conversation. If you are genuinely continuing the same task, stay in the same chat. If you are switching topics, start new.

Your First Session Checklist

Before every AI session: (1) Check protected status - verify you see the enterprise/protected indicator. (2) Start a new chat if starting a new task. (3) Choose your mode - Standard for quick tasks, Reasoning for complex analysis. (4) Be specific in your prompt - clear input leads to better output. (5) Review critically - remember the parrot sounds confident but may be wrong.

Summary

You now know how to access the tool safely, choose the right mode, and develop good habits around starting fresh conversations. In the next module, we will explore the "Red Lines" - the ethical and legal boundaries that apply even when your data is technically secure.
